title: One Day in December vs In Five Years: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Josie Silver's One Day in December vs Rebecca Serle's In Five Years—emotional romance compared. Which tearjerker should you read first?

book1.title: One Day in December
book1.author: Josie Silver
book1.description: One Day in December follows Laurie, who spots her soulmate through a London bus window but loses him in the crowd. After a year of searching, she finally meets Jack—only to discover he's her best friend Sarah's new boyfriend. Unable to reveal the truth, Laurie watches Jack and Sarah's relationship develop while suppressing her own feelings. The novel spans a decade, following Laurie and Jack's lives, relationships with other people, career changes, and the constant pull between them. It explores timing, fate, friendship loyalty, and whether soulmates are worth waiting for. The book features British charm, multiple timelines showing years passing, and the ultimate right-person-wrong-time romance.

book2.title: In Five Years
book2.author: Rebecca Serle
book2.description: In Five Years opens with Dannie Kohan, a Type-A Manhattan lawyer, having her life perfectly planned. After her boyfriend proposes, she has a vivid dream where she wakes up five years in the future, in a different apartment with a man who isn't her fiancé. Shaken but dismissive, Dannie continues her planned life—until she meets the man from her dream, her best friend Bella's boyfriend. As the five-year mark approaches, Dannie must confront whether the dream was a premonition and what it means for her meticulously planned life. The book is a twist on fate and destiny, exploring friendship, love, grief, and learning to embrace uncertainty. It's emotionally devastating with a surprise that recontextualizes everything.

table.book2: Readers wanting emotional depth and twists

book1Strengths: The decade-long yearning is exquisitely painful—you feel every missed opportunity
Jack and Laurie's chemistry is undeniable despite the impossible situation
The friendship loyalty conflict feels real—Laurie's guilt is palpable
British setting adds charm and cozy atmosphere to the heartbreak
Shows multiple relationships and life changes realistically over years
The payoff after years of waiting is incredibly satisfying
book1Weaknesses: The premise requires believing they couldn't just talk about the bus moment
Some readers find Laurie's choices frustrating—should have spoken up sooner
The decade span means some years feel rushed or summarized
Sarah's characterization can feel less developed as a potential obstacle
book2Strengths: The twist completely recontextualizes everything—genuinely shocking and devastating
Explores friendship as deeply as romance—Bella and Dannie's bond is central
Tackles grief, loss, and learning to live with uncertainty beautifully
Short and impactful—no unnecessary padding, every scene matters
The philosophical questions about fate and control are thought-provoking
Emotionally devastating in ways you don't see coming
book2Weaknesses: The twist may leave readers feeling emotionally wrecked (trigger warnings apply)
Some feel the romance is underdeveloped compared to expectations
The premonition premise requires major suspension of disbelief
Very short—some readers want more time with the characters and story

book1: You were my one day in December, and I've been looking for you ever since.
Some people are meant to fall in love with each other, but not meant to be together.
Timing is a funny thing. Sometimes it's perfect, and sometimes it ruins everything.
How do you tell your best friend you're in love with her boyfriend?
A year of searching, and I finally found you. At the worst possible time.
Maybe soulmates don't always end up together. Maybe that's the tragedy.
book2: We're not designed to see the future. And maybe that's for the best.
The thing about plans is that they never quite go the way you think they will.
In five years, I saw my future. But I didn't see the whole picture.
Some people come into our lives not to stay, but to teach us how to live.
Life is what happens when you're busy making plans.
Love isn't always romantic. Sometimes the greatest love story is a friendship.

book1: You love right-person-wrong-time romance with years of yearning
You're drawn to stories about soulmates and fate across a decade
You want British charm and London setting
You enjoy watching characters' lives evolve over long time periods
You want a satisfying romantic payoff after emotional torture
book2: You want a short, impactful book that packs emotional devastation
You're interested in philosophical questions about fate and free will
You want friendship to be as important as romance
You can handle emotionally heavy twists and grief
You appreciate books that make you think about life's uncertainty

verdict.summary: These books serve completely different emotional needs. One Day in December is pure romance—a decade-long yearning for the right person at the wrong time with a satisfying happy ending. In Five Years is more philosophical and friendship-focused, with a devastating twist that shifts the entire story away from expected romance. We'd say One Day in December is the better choice for romance readers wanting swoony yearning, while In Five Years is for readers wanting emotional depth and unexpected gut-punches. Your mood determines which is right.
verdict.recommendation: Read One Day in December if you want a proper romance with years of yearning. Laurie and Jack's bus stop moment is iconic, and watching them orbit each other for a decade while he's with her best friend is exquisitely painful. British charm, satisfying romantic payoff, and the ultimate right-person-wrong-time story. At 400 pages, you get invested in their lives. Perfect for romance fans wanting bittersweet longing with a happy ending. Read In Five Years if you want a short, devastating book about fate and friendship. At 256 pages, it's quick but packs a massive emotional punch. The twist is genuinely shocking and will wreck you. Friendship is as central as romance, and it explores grief beautifully. Be prepared for emotional devastation—not a light read. Perfect for readers wanting depth and philosophical questions. Both feature timing and fate, but One Day is romantic yearning while In Five Years is existential grief.
